question_text: Which source backbone maps to U01 - Vital Signs I: Measurement Foundations?
rationale: U01 is built from Module 10 - Vital Signs and focuses on measurement foundations, temperature, pain observation, recording, and reporting within CNA scope.

question_text: Resident A is quiet, wrapped in two blankets, states 'I feel cold,' has an oral temperature of 100.8 F at 0730, and reports right knee pain 6/10. What is the best CNA action?
answer_a: Wait until the end of shift because routine charting is enough.
answer_b: Record the reading with route and time, report the change and pain statement promptly to the licensed nurse, and document factual observations.
answer_c: Diagnose an infection and decide which pain medicine is needed.
answer_d: Enter the resident's real name and room number into the Moodle reflection.
rationale: The U01 CNA role is to measure as assigned, include route and time, report temperature and pain concerns promptly, document facts, and avoid diagnosis, medication decisions, and PHI entry.

question_text: Which action is outside CNA scope when a resident has a temperature change and pain report?
answer_a: Using the assigned pain scale and reporting the resident's statement.
answer_b: Recording an assigned temperature with the route, time, and related observations.
answer_c: Deciding the resident has an infection and changing the care plan independently.
answer_d: Protecting privacy while notifying the licensed nurse according to policy.
rationale: CNAs report measured facts, resident statements, and observations. Diagnosing infection or independently changing the care plan exceeds CNA scope.

question_text: Which charting example is most objective for the U01 scenario after the nurse is notified?
answer_a: Resident was dramatic and probably trying to avoid breakfast.
answer_b: Resident's family caused the knee problem and the fever.
answer_c: Resident is infected and needs medication now.
answer_d: 0730 oral temperature 100.8 F; resident stated 'I feel cold' and reported right knee pain 6/10 when standing; guarding observed; nurse notified.
rationale: Objective U01 documentation includes value, route, time, resident statement, pain rating, observed cue, and notification without labels, blame, diagnosis, or medication decisions.

question_text: In vital-sign terminology, what does afebrile mean?
rationale: Afebrile means without fever. U01 uses temperature terms so CNAs can communicate accurately without over-interpreting findings.

question_text: Before a shower, a resident grimaces, holds the bedrail, and reports new hip pain as 8/10 when standing. Which response follows U01 pain-observation principles?
answer_a: Tell the resident to ignore the pain because the shower schedule is busy.
answer_b: Pause the activity as safe, observe and report the pain statement and cues to the nurse, and follow direction before continuing.
answer_c: Decide the resident needs a specific medication and document that it was requested.
answer_d: Chart that pain resolved because the resident stopped standing.
rationale: Pain is the resident's report supported by CNA observations. New severe pain with movement should be reported promptly while the CNA stays within care-plan and licensed-direction boundaries.

question_text: A resident's last temperature was documented as axillary, and the current assigned reading is oral. What should the CNA include when recording or reporting the current reading?
answer_a: Only that the temperature is higher or lower than last time.
answer_b: A diagnosis explaining why the two readings differ.
answer_c: The current value, oral route, time, related observations, and notification if the finding is unusual.
answer_d: Nothing about route because all temperature routes are interchangeable.
rationale: Temperature route and time are essential measurement details. Oral and axillary readings are not automatically interchangeable, and the CNA reports facts rather than diagnoses.

question_text: Which statement about U01 completion and certificate release is compliance-safe while the course is Draft / Pending CDPH Approval?
answer_a: Passing the U01 quiz alone releases a certificate immediately.
answer_b: The U01 scenario awards clinical or simulation hours.
answer_c: Learners should upload facility vital-sign forms to prove participation.
answer_d: Certificate release remains blocked until approval metadata, required Moodle evidence, assessment gates, identity/no-PHI attestations, and administrative approval-status gates are satisfied.
rationale: U01 is online CE only. Certificates must remain unavailable until all required evidence and approval-status conditions are satisfied; clinical/simulation claims and copied forms are excluded.

question_text: In a U01 sorting activity, which item belongs in the category 'do not chart as a diagnosis'?
answer_a: Resident has an infection causing the fever.
answer_d: Nurse notified of temperature and right knee pain 6/10.
rationale: Saying the resident has an infection is a diagnosis. CNAs should chart the measured temperature, resident quote, pain report, observations, and notification.

question_text: When an unusual temperature or pain observation is found, which statement best reflects U01 reporting foundations?
answer_a: Routine charting always replaces direct reporting to the nurse.
answer_b: Report promptly according to policy, recheck only as directed, document facts with route/time or pain scale, and include who was notified.
answer_c: Delay reporting until the resident has the same finding on three shifts.
answer_d: Post a de-identified story online and ask classmates what diagnosis fits.
rationale: U01 emphasizes timely reporting, accurate measurement details, factual documentation, privacy, and CNA scope. Rechecking should follow direction and should not delay urgent reporting.
